title: Arrays and Lists
summary: Store collections of items in lists and access, add, or remove elements

sections.title: Lists are like a numbered to-do list
sections.content: A Python list is an ordered collection of items, just like a grocery list on your fridge. Each item has a position (index) starting at 0 — so the first item is at position 0, the second at position 1, and so on. This zero-based counting feels odd at first, but it's standard across programming. Lists can hold any data type: strings, numbers, booleans, or even other lists. You create one with square brackets and commas between items: ['apples', 'bananas', 'milk'].

sections.title: Working with a list of student scores
sections.content: This example creates a list of test scores, accesses individual scores, and adds a new one
sections.code:
# Create a list of four test scores using square brackets
scores = [85, 92, 78, 95]  # Four students' test results
# Access the first score — index 0 is always the first element
first_score = scores[0]  # Gets 85 from position 0
# Access the last score — index -1 wraps to the last element
last_score = scores[-1]  # Gets 95 from the end of the list
# Add a new score to the end of the list using append()
scores.append(88)  # Now the list has 5 elements: [85, 92, 78, 95, 88]
# Get the total number of items in the list using len()
number_of_scores = len(scores)  # Returns 5
print("First:", first_score, "| Last:", last_score)
print("Total scores:", number_of_scores)
print("All scores:", scores)
# Expected output:
# First: 85 | Last: 95
# Total scores: 5
# All scores: [85, 92, 78, 95, 88]

sections.title: List operations you'll use every day
sections.content: Beyond accessing individual items, lists support powerful operations. You can slice a list to get a portion — like getting items 1 through 3 with scores[1:4]. The append() method adds one item to the end. remove() deletes a specific value. pop() removes and returns the last item. You can check if something is in a list with the 'in' keyword: if 'Milk' in groceries:. Lists are mutable — you can change, add, or remove items after creation. This flexibility makes lists one of Python's most-used features.

sections.title: Index out of range errors
sections.content: Accessing an index that doesn't exist crashes your program. Wrong: items = ['a', 'b']; print(items[5]) raises IndexError. Always check that the index is less than the length. Correct: if 5 < len(items): print(items[5]). Or use negative indices to access from the end safely: items[-1] gives the last item without knowing the length.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: You're tracking daily temperatures for a week. Create a list with 7 temperature values (any numbers). Write code to: print the third day's temperature, add an 8th day, and print how many temperatures you have in total.
hints: What index corresponds to the third item in a list?
Which method adds a new element to the end of a list?
